overview.title: Introduction
overview.body: God’s dream—expressed through the commissioning vision of Jesus’ final words on earth—is to see every expression of humanity, both individual and corporate, restored by the Spirit of God through the redemptive message of the Gospel. He wants every person to have the opportunity to be experience God’s life and be adopted into the community of the family of God (Mar 16:15). He wants every nation to be able to experience the transformative impact of the kingdom of God in every dimension of its cultural life, shaping every sphere of society. This is a compelling dream; a dream which calls us to love God with all our heart, soul, mind and strength until it has become an experiential reality here on earth. Until then we work and pray as he taught us, “your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as it is in heaven” (Mat 6:10 NIV).

overview.title: God’s Purpose
overview.body: God is the one who had the original idea of family. He instituted marriage at the very beginning of creation (Gen 2:24) as the life-time commitment between one man and one woman. When the original couple expanded into a family with the birth of their first child we are told that it was “with the Lord’s help” (Gen 4:1). His purpose for this sphere is that life might be multiplied and that a God-given destiny for every individual may be established. God wants us to engage with families, to serve and strengthen them, to see his purposes of life and destiny restored in them in order to serve an emerging generation.

overview.title: Methodology
overview.body: The SphereView highlights in the Bible are designed to help you see everything related to family in the Bible. This includes prescriptive passages (those which instruct us how things should be–things like “husbands love your wives”) and descriptive passages (those which tell us how things actually were–stories like that of Cain killing his brother Abel). Our research highlighted all kinds of family events: weddings, births, and funerals to name a few. Stories of love, romance, and sex–both appropriate and inappropriate–are included in the family highlights. So we highlight passages that speak of husbands, wives, concubines, prostitutes, parents, children, brothers, sisters, uncles, and aunts. And of course, we highlight all the genealogies for they tell the multi-generational story of how families grow to be clans then tribes and finally nations. More than one fourth of the Bible speaks of family, underscoring how foundational it is to society.

overview.title: God’s Purpose
overview.body: Economics includes both science and technology together with the arena of business. God’s purpose for the sphere is to release provision and model stewardship. Science is the generative motor of this sphere, for it produces the research and development that allows for the creation of wealth by unlocking new products and services. It is then propelled forward by the distributive capacity of the business enterprise which produces and sells those inventions. When stewarded well, this process of creating and distributing wealth should glorify the Creator who works with humans as co-creators in the desire to multiply life-enhancing innovations.

overview.title: Methodology
overview.body: From Eden until now God has given human beings productive tasks to accomplish. This sphere is very broad. It encompasses much of the human experience, a fact which is reflected in the breadth of the SphereView highlights. With science and technology in mind we highlight passages of research, innovation and creativity (both God’s and ours). With business in mind we highlight all the many professional expressions found in the Bible: those of shepherds, carpenters, farmers, builders, fishermen, manufacturers, merchants, etc. Stories of buying and selling as well as instructions about how we should deal with land, wealth, money and capital resources are all highlighted. Attitudes and actions related to the rich and the poor are likewise highlighted. You may be amazed to see how many parables of Jesus use economic images to teach about the kingdom of God. There is much to explore, so enjoy the journey of discovery.

overview.title: God’s Purpose
overview.body: God has appointed government in order to safeguard justice and create a peaceful and safe environment within the nation. Rulers should exercise delegated authority in order to serve the citizens whom they govern. The “Law of the King” found in Deuteronomy 17:14-21 warns those in authority against multiplying that which would lead them into a life-style of authoritarianism, hedonism and materialism. God’s alternative to these three unrighteous value systems is servanthood, purity and generosity. But since we are aware that our civic leaders are often pressed to compromise in the areas of power, sex and money, we must pray and work to see every form of injustice, corruption and greed brought to an end.

overview.title: Methodology
overview.body: This sphere has the second most highlights of any in the Bible. The highlights include everything that has to do with kings, queens, other royals, governors and their representatives, nations along with their territories and people, soldiers and guards, generals and other military officers, war and peace, ambassadors, judges, lawmakers, tax-collectors and other civic officials. There are stories of governmental intrigue, adversaries vying for political power, coup d’etats and governmental assassinations. Concepts of citizenship and statements about foreigners and refugees are also a part of this sphere. Passages that speak of the homeland and patriotism are highlighted as well. There are clear precepts for government leaders and those that follow them; and of course there are an abundance of stories: some stories are told as examples for us to imitate; others are warnings that speak of events which we should avoid.

overview.title: God’s Purpose
overview.body: God’s purpose for the sphere of religion is the extension of mercy and the promotion of reconciliation between those who have had relationships broken. This is to occur horizontally between human beings, and vertically between individuals and God. Competing religious worldviews contend for the hearts and minds of men and women everywhere. Without falling into a contentious spirit, we are to enter this marketplace of religious ideas and clearly display why the gospel of Christ is unique. We are called to be models of this wonderful message of good news, which is the only means of enduring grace, for it alone makes lasting reconciliation possible through the sacrificial love displayed on the cross.

overview.title: Methodology
overview.body: This is the most highlighted sphere in the Bible – occupying more than three-fifths of the Biblical text. This is not surprising for we usually consider The Bible a religious book and everything related to God as religious. But what we discover in the SphereView Bible is that God relates to us within the framework of all seven spheres, not just the religious one. So what have we highlighted that is specifically religious? Those passages that speak of God’s revelation, redemption and reconciliation. We highlight all human religious endeavor whether righteous or idolatrous. Precepts of mercy and forgiveness, the transforming impact of the gospel. God’s presence – His ‘Immanuelness’ – and His covenants are also areas that are highlighted. Events in the tabernacle, the temple, and the church, as well as passages of sacrifice and worship are all included.

overview.title: God’s Purpose
overview.body: The purpose of discipleship is the transformation of the student rather than the mere transaction of knowledge. God’s plan for education is the discipleship and multiplication of understanding, values and skills applied in a way that enables each student to discover and develop their gifts which can be used to contribute to society in a beneficial way. With this perspective education becomes a means of developing Biblical Christians who have a strong sense of identity and calling, with Jesus as their model and the Bible as their foundation. True education should occur in a family-friendly context, for God has delegated to the family the primary authority and responsibility for the education of their children. Every family on earth should have access to a Christ-centered education for their children.

overview.title: Methodology
overview.body: In light of the fact that “All Scripture is inspired by God and is useful to teach us what is true” (2Ti 3:16), we could have highlighted every word in the Bible, but that would not have served you by helping you focus on educational insights. So we have highlighted all those key words that indicate an intentional educational process is at work. Every word that gives us an explanation as to why something is as it is, thus cultivating critical thinking (words like ‘because, for, since’) are highlighted because education is happening right then and there. Similarly, words that explain purpose and implicational thinking (words like ‘that, so that, in order that, therefore’) are highlighted. Likewise every question in the Bible is highlighted, for the one who asks is desiring to gain new information or insight; to be educated. Add to these the many stories of revelation, study, discovery, and discipleship – and then you have much to explore.

overview.title: God’s Purpose
overview.body: God’s purposes for the sphere of communication media is the transfer of wisdom and the promotion of healthy relationships. Proverbs is full of pithy axioms which emphasize the power of communication to do good or to do ill. There is great power not only in what is said but in how it is said. Today, with new media technologies emerging one after another, we have more and more capacity to communicate than ever before. But what are we communicating? Is it bringing life or death? Is it enhancing wisdom or promoting folly? We must enter into this arena as followers of Jesus to use every tool available to advance the ways of the kingdom of God.

overview.title: Methodology
overview.body: The whole of the Bible is an amazing communication tool, so we could have highlighted every single word, but to what purpose? Instead we have highlighted all those phrases which alert you to the acts of communication–myriads of simple statements like: he said, showed, promised, listened, praised, swore, watched, yelled, demonstrated, responded, cried, spoke, whispered, etc. We have highlighted every form of communication possible: verbal and non-verbal, personal responses and cultural protocols, visual, tactile, dramatic. These many expressions sometimes take place in vertical relationships (between God and humans) and sometimes in horizontal ones (between humans). Communication highlights cover both private conversations and public media – which in Biblical times included the use of trumpets, pageantry, incense, and other expressions. These many examples of communication are also accentuated with teachings about what is and what is not godly communication.

overview.title: God’s Purpose
overview.body: This sphere includes all the arts, areas of entertainment and sports. God’s purposes for the sphere of celebration is to strengthen hope and build identity and community. Ever since the days of Jubal (Gen 4:21), balladeers and artists have used their skills to entertain people and speak to the heart of their culture. In the Bible, these artistic gifts are often identified with the prophetic ministry. When godly ideas are embedded within a creative expression, the truth resonates with both heart and mind, maximizing the potential impact in the life of those who are drawn into the story. Stories of God’s work in the past build hope for the future, giving strength and courage to face the ever present challenges of life’s journey.

overview.title: Methodology
overview.body: Though this is the sphere with the least amount of highlights in the Bible you will still be amazed at the great breadth of coverage it receives in the Scriptures. Many expressions of art are highlighted: the fine arts, painting, sculpture, the performing arts, dance, theater, various kinds of crafts, pottery-making, textile arts, the art of the jeweler, culinary arts, leather work, fashion and couture. These arts are some times used for godly worship, sometimes for idolatrous devotion. Many communal events are spoken of in the Bible, commemorating births, weddings, funerals, victories, defeats – these are occasions for celebration or lamentation. These are highlighted along with those passages which describe cultural protocols, various festivals, expressions of hospitality, and sporting events.
